narrative:
Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates applicants based on their likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 48% probability that you would fail it. This prediction draws from patterns in data about thousands of past students, including their academic records, test scores, family backgrounds, and more, to help the school make informed admission decisions. For you, as a Black male with a lower-middle family income background, the model zeroed in on a few key aspects of your profile that shaped this outcome.

The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward bar exam failure was your LSAT score of 24, which is notably below the typical applicant's average of around 37 and stood out as the strongest signal of potential struggle ahead. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.60—while under the average of 3.21—worked in your favor, pulling the prediction somewhat toward success by showing solid college performance relative to what the model expects. Your family income bracket also helped your case, as it nudged the odds slightly toward passing, countering some of the other concerns.

Compared to typical students, your Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles (1st and 3rd, respectively, versus averages around 5th-6th) suggest early academic challenges that the model noted but didn't weigh as heavily here, and your plan to attend full-time aligns with the vast majority of successful applicants. Overall, the low LSAT overshadowed these strengths, tipping the balance to a rejection threshold. While this is disappointing, especially given the barriers many Black students like yourself face in accessing top-tier prep resources, it doesn't define your potential.

To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the average, as that's the single biggest lever you can pull based on how the model weighs it. Strengthening your application with additional experiences, like targeted bar prep courses or leadership roles, could also help in reapplying to other programs. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these gaps—keep pushing, and reach out to advisors for personalized strategies.
